name: Soviet SSH-40 Helmet
description:
Soviet SSH-40 steel helmet from World War II, the standard issue helmet
for Red Army forces during the Great Patriotic War. This example was
manufactured in 1942 during the height of the conflict.

The SSH-40 was an improvement over the earlier SSH-39, featuring a
simplified production process while maintaining excellent protection.
The distinctive shape with its wide brim provided good coverage.

This helmet retains its original khaki green paint with visible wear
from field use. The three-pad liner system and chinstrap are intact.
Factory markings stamped inside indicate Leningrad production.

name: British Paratrooper Beret
description:
Authentic World War II British Airborne Forces maroon beret. This iconic
"Red Beret" was worn by paratroopers of the British Army during operations
including Market Garden and the Rhine crossings.

Features the original cap badge of the Parachute Regiment with the distinctive
Bellerophon riding Pegasus design. The interior retains its leather sweatband
and manufacturer's label.

name: US M1 Steel Helmet
description:
World War II era US M1 steel helmet, the iconic "steel pot" that served
American forces from 1941 through the 1980s. This example dates to 1943
production and features the characteristic olive drab finish.

The helmet consists of two parts: the outer steel shell and the inner
fiber liner (M1 helmet liner). This example retains both components
along with the original webbing suspension system and chin strap.

Front seam construction indicates early war production. Heat stamp
visible inside the shell shows manufacturer and lot number.

name: German M35 Steel Helmet
description:
Original World War II German M35 Stahlhelm (steel helmet) in excellent condition.
This helmet features the classic Germanic design with rolled edge and original
field grey paint finish. The interior retains its original leather liner and
aluminum liner band. Chinstrap is complete with original buckle.

Markings visible inside include the manufacturer's stamp and lot number.
This example shows typical wartime wear consistent with field use.
